frameworks.name: low-level spatial vision
frameworks.one_line: shared spatial mechanisms underlie both object-distance and interpersonal-distance estimation

frameworks.name: geometric visual illusion
frameworks.one_line: Müller-Lyer illusion extended from line stimuli to social figure pairs

frameworks.name: social scene perception
frameworks.one_line: proxemic cues inform inferences about interaction and relationship

core_question: Does the Müller-Lyer illusion distort perceived distance between two human figures the same way it distorts non-social line distances?
key_assumptions: interpersonal distance is a computed visual feature, not only a social inference
Müller-Lyer fins act on figure endpoints analogously to line endpoints
psychophysical estimates index the same spatial mechanism across stimulus classes
manipulations: Müller-Lyer fin configuration flanking pairs of human figures
social vs non-social stimulus type (figures vs classical lines)
three psychophysical experiments varying display parameters
key_findings: observers mis-estimated interpersonal distance in the direction predicted by Müller-Lyer
illusion magnitude for figure pairs was comparable to that for non-social stimuli
effect was consistent across all three experiments
interpretations: interpersonal-distance perception relies on the same low-level spatial mechanisms as classical distance perception
social scene appraisal inherits geometric biases from generic visual distance computation
